mission.title: Install and Configure Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
mission.description: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment, management, and operations of Kubernetes clusters in Azure. It is ideal for users looking for a fully managed solution with integrated Azure services, but it may incur additional costs compared to self-managed solutions.

steps.title: Install Azure CLI
steps.description:
The Azure CLI is required to interact with Azure resources. This command installs the Azure CLI on your local machine.

```bash

curl -sL https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCLIDeb | sudo bash

```

> For Debian-based systems.

steps.title: Create an AKS Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new AKS cluster with the specified Kubernetes version, node count, and region.

```bash

az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus

```

> Replace 'myResourceGroup' and 'myAKSCluster' with your desired names.
commands: az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus

steps.title: Configure kubectl
steps.description:
This command configures kubectl to use the new AKS cluster by updating the kubeconfig file.

```bash

az aks get-credentials --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster

```

> This allows you to interact with your AKS cluster using kubectl.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your AKS cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes listed with a 'Ready' status.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
This step sets up monitoring and autoscaling for your AKS cluster.

```bash

az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

az aks update --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --enable-cluster-autoscaler --min-count 1 --max-count 5

```

> This enables Azure Monitor for containers.
> This configures cluster autoscaling.
commands: az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

uninstall.title: Delete the AKS Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the AKS cluster and all associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss and orphaned resources.

```bash

az aks delete --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --yes --no-wait

```

> This will remove the cluster and associated resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade AKS Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up any critical data. This command upgrades the AKS cluster to the specified version.

```bash

az aks upgrade --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --kubernetes-version 1.31

kubectl get nodes

```

> Replace with the desired version.
> Verify that the nodes are updated and in 'Ready' status.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create the AKS cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to insufficient permissions or quota limits in your Azure subscription.

**Fix:**
Check your Azure subscription permissions and ensure you have enough quota for the requested resources.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'kubectl: Unable to connect to the server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that kubectl is not configured correctly or the AKS cluster is down.

**Fix:**
Run 'az aks get-credentials --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster' to reconfigure kubectl.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This happens when the cluster does not have enough resources to schedule new pods.

**Fix:**
Scale up your node count using 'az aks scale --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count <new-count>'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Pod is in CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that a pod is failing to start due to application errors or misconfigurations.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs using 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' to diagnose the issue.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new Azure regions and improved integration with Azure Monitor.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.29, ensure all custom resources are compatible with the new version.

resolution.summary: You should see a list of nodes with 'Ready' status when running 'kubectl get nodes'.
codeSnippets: az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus
az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the Azure CLI and kubectl installed on your local machine.

az monitor autoscale create --resource-group myResourceGroup --resource myAKSCluster --resource-type Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters --name myAutoscale --min-count 1 --max-count 5 --count 3

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the Azure CLI and kubectl installed and configured.
